input_path: data\papers\Mertens and Denil - 2025 - Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study: The physical system is a lab-scale gantry crane with a controller, designed for research and educational purposes. It is publicly available, uses freely available software, and serves as a reproducible exemplar for validating digital twin methodologies such as continuous validation, fault detection, and system evolution notation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_services: The Digital Twin provides services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization, and continuous validation. These services enable real-time monitoring, predictive analysis, and validation of system behavior through bi-directional data flow between the physical and digital components, supporting researchers and educators in digital twin research and education.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers: The Digital Twin implementation uses Python (gantrylib) for system control and data processing, Docker for containerization, TimescaleDB for time-series data storage, Grafana for data visualization, Eclipse Mosquitto as the MQTT Broker for messaging, Arduino for hardware interfacing, Autodesk Fusion360 for CAD modeling, Capella for system architecture modeling, and MkDocs for documentation.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation: The system uses the C4 Model for architecture visualization with context and container diagrams, employing containerization technologies for deployment and following an ad-hoc approach without a commercial digital twin framework.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data: The Digital Twin system employs CAD models for geometric representation and a kinematic model for motion simulation. Data management utilizes a TimescaleDB database to store all operational data. Key services include the Trajectory Generator Service, which leverages the kinematic model to compute movement paths, and the Simulation Service for executing dynamic simulations of the crane's behavior.
extracted_characteristics.physical_sensing_components: Sensing infrastructure comprises a Swing Angle Encoder to measure hoist swinging motion, an Anemometer for real-time wind speed monitoring, and an Arduino microcontroller for data acquisition and processing. Sensors are strategically placed to capture critical environmental and mechanical parameters for feedback control.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: Data from the Physical Crane is collected via the Physical Crane Interface (Python program) and continuously logged to TimescaleDB by the Logging Application. Specific data types, communication protocols, transmission frequencies, and preprocessing steps are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.virtual_to_physical_interaction: Control commands are issued through the Human Machine Interface (HMI), processed by the Control Application (Python), and executed via the Physical Crane Interface. Specific command types, message formats, validation mechanisms, and execution confirmation details are not provided.
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: MQTT Broker is used for signaling between services including Trajectory Generator, Simulation Service, and Continuous Validation Service. TimescaleDB serves as the data storage. Network topology, bandwidth, latency, reliability, redundancy, and security protocols are not specified.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: All software runs on a personal computer, and the Database, MQTT Broker, and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker. Specific computational and storage requirements, scalability mechanisms, and deployment models are not detailed in the document.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Air Quality Management exemplar uses Model-Driven digital twin engineering methodologies, with version control via GitHub and containerization/virtualization for code dissemination. The Lab Scale Gantry Crane applies the C4 Model for architecture visualization. All exemplars adopt an ad-hoc approach rather than commercial digital twin frameworks.
extracted_characteristics.insights_and_decision_making: The Air Quality Management digital twin provides runtime visualization, simulation, and prediction capabilities for indoor air quality management in virus spread prevention scenarios.
extracted_characteristics.standardization: AutomationML standards are used for the digital twin model in the Air Quality Management exemplar, while the Lab Scale Gantry Crane employs the C4 Model for software architecture visualization.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
# Lab-Scale Gantry Crane Digital Twin Exemplar



Joost Mertens

_Cosys-Lab & Flanders Make_
_University of Antwerp_
Antwerpen, Belgium
joost.mertens@uantwerpen.be


_**Abstract**_ **—The research topic of digital twins has attracted a**
**large amount of interest over the past decade. However, publicly**
**available exemplars remain scarce. In the interest of open and**
**reproducible science, in this exemplar paper we present a lab-**
**scale gantry crane and its digital twin. The exemplar comprises**
**both the physical and digital side of the twin system. The physical**
**side consists of the physical crane and its controller. The digital**
**side covers the CAD models and kinematic model of the crane,**
**and provides services for optimal control, historical data logging,**
**data visualization and continuous validation. We used this setup**
**as use case in several previous publications where its functionality**
**was validated. It is publicly available and only relies on other**
**freely available and commonly used software, this way we hope**
**it can be used for future research or education on the topic of**
**digital twins.**
_**Index Terms**_ **—exemplar, digital twin, open-source, cyber-**
**physical-system**


I. I NTRODUCTION

The digital twin paradigm connects an actual system with a
digital counterpart consisting of models of parts of the actual
systems and services derived therefrom. During operation
data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the
twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual
system’s workings. While the paradigm is applicable for both
physical and digital actual objects [1], in practice we see it
mainly applied to physical ones [2], [3].
Because of this bi-directional interaction, demonstrating
research results requires relevant use cases. Such research
results could be new synchronization methods, better or faster
data streaming, improved services and the like. The exemplar
in this paper for example, was used as a demonstrator for the
following digital twin related research: continuous validation
methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a
notation for system evolution [7].
In our experience, the use cases themselves typically do
not contain new research, yet they demand a substantial development effort on the researcher’s part. Finding of-the-shelf
exemplars in literature is rare, as the use cases in papers are
often not described in a reproducible manner, are costly to set
up, or are not openly available because they contain intellectual
property. Having readily available off-the-shelf exemplars in
various domains would help reduce development overhead,
and additionally also also help support the generalizability of
research results.

Given the industry relevance of digital twins, education
is another field for which well described exemplars can be



Joachim Denil

block_1_judge.reasoning: The description accurately identifies the system as a lab-scale gantry crane with a controller, intended for research and education. It correctly notes the system is publicly available, uses freely available software, and has been used to validate specific methodologies (continuous validation, fault detection, system evolution). Supported by quotes: 'we present a lab-scale gantry crane and its digital twin', 'The physical side consists of the physical crane and its controller', 'It is publicly available and only relies on other freely available and commonly used software', 'demonstrator for the following digital twin related research: continuous validation methods [4], [5], fault detection methods [6] and DarTwin, a notation for system evolution [7]'.

The description correctly lists the main services: optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization, and continuous validation. It also correctly describes the bi-directional data flow and the benefit to researchers and educators. However, it mentions 'predictive analysis' which is not explicitly listed as a service in the text (though a simulation service exists, its explicit link to predictive analysis is not detailed in the provided snippets), and 'real-time monitoring' is implied but not explicitly detailed as a service name. The core services are accurate. Supported by quotes: 'provides services for optimal control, historical data logging, data visualization and continuous validation', 'During operation data flows from the actual to the digital system to feed the twin’s services. In turn, the twin’s services improve the actual system’s workings.'

This extraction is excellent. It provides a highly specific and complete list of the technologies used (Python/gantrylib, Docker, TimescaleDB, Grafana, Eclipse Mosquitto, Arduino, Autodesk Fusion360, Capella, MkDocs) and correctly attributes their specific functionalities based on the 'Architecture', 'Implementation', and 'Repository Organization' sections.

block_2_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_

block_3_judge.reasoning: The extraction accurately identifies the CAD models, kinematic model, TimescaleDB database, and specific services like the Trajectory Generator and Simulation Service, all of which are explicitly detailed in the source text.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents explicitly describe physical actuators and control mechanisms. The text details motors, controllers, and an electromagnet. Quotes: 'Two Motors and associated Controllers, one to control the cart’s movement, one to control the hoisting movement', 'An Electromagnet at the end of the hoist to pick up containers'.

Accurately lists the Swing Angle Encoder, Anemometer, and Arduino microcontroller for data acquisition, as described in the text. The description includes some interpretive language about placement and feedback control that is not explicitly supported by the snippets, but the core components are correct.

The extraction states 'Not in Document', but the source documents describe a Continuous Validation Service that performs verification by comparing measurements with simulations and using distance metrics. Quotes: 'Continuous Validation Service: a service that continuously compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations', 'It calculates distance metrics between them and compares the value with an acceptable threshold'.

block_4_docs_retrieved:
III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_


Figure 2 shows the context diagram of the system. In
essence, it shows the high-level abstraction of what a digital
twin is, and two actors that interact with the system. The
operator is the actor that would operate and monitor this
system day to day, whilst the researcher can perform digital
twin research on it.
Figure 3 shows the container [6] diagram of the system. We
have limited ourselves to the most necessary relationships to
keep the diagram readable.
The gantry crane system, which can operate without digital
twin if needed, consists of five containers.
**Physical Crane:** the actual hardware of the crane.


5 [https://c4model.com/](https://c4model.com/)
6 Not a Docker container, a container is an abstraction in the C4 model for
something that must be there for the system to work.


Fig. 3. Container diagram of the system.



**Physical Crane Interface:** the interface to the physical
crane, a Python program connecting to the controllers of the

crane.

**Control Application:** a Python application that uses the
crane interface to control the crane.

**Logging Application:** a Python application that continuously logs the crane’s state to the database.
**Human Machine Interface:** a Python front-end for the
operator to configure and control the crane.
The digital twin consists of six containers. We’ll describe
them bottom up.

**Database:** a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data

created by the system and its various services.
**Dashboard:** a Grafana dashboard that visualizes data from

the database.

**Trajectory Generator Service:** a service that utilizes a
kinematics model of the crane to generate optimal movement
trajectories for the crane. Such optimal trajectories have no
residual swinging motion at the end.
**Simulation Service:** a service that implements a simulation
of the crane and it’s controllers. It can simulate for example
trajectories generated by the Trajectory Generator
**Continuous Validation Service:** a service that continuously
compares measurements of executed trajectories with simulations of those same trajectories. It calculates distance metrics
between them and compares the value with an acceptable
threshold. In case the threshold is breached operators are
notified via the dashboard and can take appropriate action.
**MQTT Broker:** an MQTT broker for signaling between
services.

Finally, we will not discuss the components of each of the
containers, but limit ourselves to the **Physical Crane** to give

Supported by quotes: 'MQTT Broker: an MQTT broker for signaling between services.' and 'Database: a TimescaleDB database that contains all the data created by the system'.

The extraction precisely captures the deployment strategy described: running all software on a personal computer with specific components containerized via Docker. It correctly identifies the lack of scalability details in the document. Supported by quotes: 'run all of the software on that computer', 'Database, MQTT Broker and Dashboard C4 Containers are containerized with Docker', 'docker compose file is provided to bring the containers up and down'.

block_5_docs_retrieved:
_B. GreenhouseDT_


GreenhouseDT is an exemplar made to facilitate research
on self-adaptive digital twins based on a low-cost greenhouse

[12]. It features behavioral adaptation of both the actual
system (the greenhouse) and the digital twin. The exemplar
and its accompanying documentation is hosted on GitHub [3] . It
relies on commonly used technologies such as an InfluxDB
database, an ActiveMQ message broker and virtual machines
for easy installation. The virtual machine images come with
prerecorded data.


_C. Air Quality Management_


The Air Quality Management exemplar was created to
research challenges in the field of Model-Driven digital twin
engineering [13]. It focuses on a construction engineering case
of indoor air quality management in the context of virus spread
prevention. Three example applications of the digital twin are
provided: runtime visualization, simulation and prediction. The
exemplar and instructions on how to set it up are hosted on
GitHub [4] . It features a Raspberry-Pi with a set of sensors for the
actual system and an AutomationML based digital twin [14].
Microsoft Azure tools are used to implement the presented
digital twin pipeline.


_D. Discussion_


The three exemplars demonstrate the use of digital twins
in the robotics, agricultural and construction domains. They
commonly use single board computers such as the RaspberryPi and containerization or virtualization technologies for easy
dissemination of the code. Interestingly, only one of them uses


2 [https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X](https://github.com/cau-se/ARCHES-PiCar-X)
3 [https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT](https://github.com/smolang/GreenhouseDT)
4 [https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality DigitalTwin Exemplar](https://github.com/derlehner/IndoorAirQuality_DigitalTwin_Exemplar)























Fig. 2. Context diagram of the system.


a commercial digital twin framework, while the others are
more ad-hoc.

In this exemplar, similar design choices (e.g. use of containerization) are also made, and we utilize a more or less
ad-hoc approach rather than using a digital twin framework.
The case itself does stem from a different domain, it is more
logistics and cyber-physical systems related, which in our
opinion makes it a good addition to these existing exemplars.


III. L AB S CALE G ANTRY C RANE


The lab scale gantry crane is a small gantry crane system
inspired by the harbor cranes in the port of Antwerp. It moves
small pucks which represents containers from one location to
another.
In what follows, we use the C4 Model [5] to visualize and
describe the architecture of the system with a context and
container diagram. Although it’s tailored for software architectures, we still find it applicable for the gantry crane system.


_A. Architecture_
